metadata.summary: Six-step process to decide what ships, when, and how it is announced. Goal, contents, readiness, communication, execution, post-release.

content.title: Release Planning
content.summary: Six-step process for deciding what goes into each release, when it ships, and how it is communicated. Covers release-type taxonomy (major/minor/patch/hotfix per SemVer 2.0), readiness checklists, rollback requirements, and audience-specific communication plans. Concrete rule: every release needs a goal statement, a one-command rollback plan, and a communication plan before code touches production.

why.p: Releases without structure fail in three predictable ways: scope creep slips into the bundle and breaks freeze, staging is skipped and integration bugs hit production, communication is missing and users notice silent changes that drown support. A structured release plan turns each shipment into a small, reversible, observable event with a known audience.

This methodology aligns with SemVer 2.0 (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH), Keep a Changelog 1.1.0, and DORA's four key metrics — deployment frequency, lead time for changes, change failure rate, time to restore service — which together correlate with elite engineering performance.

list.item: Planning what to include in an upcoming version (major, minor, patch, or hotfix).
Coordinating release timing across engineering, support, and marketing for a SaaS or library.
Writing release notes for an existing set of merged changes that have not yet shipped.
Setting up a repeatable release cadence for a solo founder or small team.
Triggers: tag matches `v*`, CHANGELOG `[Unreleased]` is non-empty, milestone closes, customer-visible breaking change is queued.
Continuous deployment with feature flags — individual flag rollouts replace release bundling.
Pre-launch products with no users — use a task list, not a release plan.
Emergency hotfixes under time pressure — jump straight to the hotfix subset (steps 3, 5, 6 only).
Internal-only refactors with no observable surface area — push through the regular CI/CD pipeline.

section.name: release-types
section.p: Release type drives cadence, risk posture, and announcement scope. Use SemVer 2.0 numbering: MAJOR for breaking, MINOR for features, PATCH for fixes. Hotfix is a PATCH bumped out-of-cadence to address a production incident.

list.item: major (vX.0.0) — quarterly or yearly. Breaking API changes, large features. Risk: high. Requires migration guide, deprecation notice ≥ one MINOR before, and beta period.
minor (v0.Y.0) — monthly or biweekly. Backward-compatible features. Risk: medium. Requires release notes and customer email.
patch (v0.0.Z) — weekly or as fixes accumulate. Bug fixes and security patches only. Risk: low. Requires changelog entry only.
hotfix (v0.0.Z+1) — within hours. Critical production fix. Risk: very low if scope is limited to the single bug. Requires post-incident review.

how-to.step: Step 1 — Define release goal. Write a single-sentence outcome the release delivers, a measurable success metric, and an optional theme. This is the title and abstract of every other artifact.

Step 2 — Select release contents. List all merge-ready changes; cut anything that is not at definition of done; freeze the list. Anything added after this point requires either an explicit re-freeze or a slot in the next release.
Step 3 — Assess readiness. Run the seven-item readiness checklist below. If any item fails, the release does not ship.
Step 4 — Plan communication. For each audience write the asset they receive (release notes, blog, email, in-app banner, social post, status page).
Step 5 — Execute. Follow the deploy ordering: final staging test → maintenance window notice (if needed) → production deploy → smoke verification → monitor metrics 30 minutes → public announcement.
Step 6 — Post-release. Watch error rates, latency, and key conversion metrics for 24 hours. Collect feedback. Run a 30-minute retrospective. Update roadmap.

rule.statement: Every release MUST have a stated user outcome, one success metric, and a target version. Use template: "Release vX.Y.Z. Goal: [outcome]. Metric: [how measured, target, deadline]. Theme: [optional narrative]."
rule.rationale: Without a goal, a release is just a list of changes. The goal connects the release to product strategy and makes the post-release retrospective meaningful. The metric forces the team to define success before shipping, not after.

rule.statement: Include only: (a) features at definition of done with passing tests and updated docs, (b) bug fixes related to the release theme or marked release-critical, (c) documentation and configuration updates that ship with the code. Exclude: half-finished features, unrelated bug fixes, scope creep added after freeze, vendored dependency upgrades not validated in staging.
rule.rationale: Half-finished features create technical debt and confuse users. Scope creep after freeze is the top empirical cause of delayed and broken releases — DORA reports correlate freeze discipline with low change-failure rate.

rule.statement: All seven readiness items MUST pass before deployment. Treat the checklist as a gate, not a guideline.
rule.rationale: Missing any one item creates a predictable class of post-release incident. The rollback plan especially: without it every incident response begins with a debate instead of an action.

checklist.check: Code complete: all PRs targeting this release are merged to the release branch and CI is green.
Tests passing: unit + integration + e2e on the release branch within the last 24 hours.
Documentation updated: README, API docs, CHANGELOG `[Unreleased]` has all entries, migration notes for breaking changes.
Migrations tested: schema migrations applied and rolled back successfully against a copy of production.
Rollback plan exists: a one-command revert path is written, owned, and tested in staging.
Support team briefed: known issues, FAQ, and triage runbook delivered to support 24 hours before deploy.
Release notes written: customer-facing notes drafted in the audience's voice and reviewed by one other person.

rule.statement: Identify every audience (internal eng, support, sales, customers, partners, ecosystem) and ship a matching asset for each: release notes, blog/changelog, customer email, social post, in-app notification, status page entry. Announce every release, including patches.
rule.rationale: Silent releases generate confused support tickets from users who noticed a change and cannot find context. The marginal cost of a one-line changelog entry is far smaller than the support volume it prevents.

checklist.check: Customer email drafted and queued in the marketing tool.
Blog post or changelog page published on release day.
Social post (X / LinkedIn) scheduled for one hour after deploy verification.
In-app banner copy and date set in the feature-flag or CMS.
Support macro updated with the new feature talk-track.
Status page incident pre-drafted in case of rollback (kept private until needed).

rule.statement: Execute strictly in order: final staging test → maintenance window announcement (if applicable) → production deploy → smoke verification → 30-minute metric watch → public release announcement. Do not announce before verifying. Do not deploy on Friday or the day before a public holiday.
rule.rationale: Order matters because a wrong-order release creates a second corrective announcement ("actually it is delayed") that erodes trust. Friday deploys multiply incident response time because weekend on-call has reduced coverage.

rule.statement: Monitor for 24 hours. Compare error rate, p95 latency, and the release's primary success metric against the prior 7-day baseline. Hold a 30-minute retrospective within 72 hours. Update roadmap and backlog with what was learned.
rule.rationale: Issues spike in the first 24 hours; early monitoring contains blast radius. Retrospectives close the learning loop that improves the next release. DORA's mean-time-to-restore metric improves directly with disciplined post-release monitoring.

section.name: commands
section.p: Reference commands an autonomous agent can run when planning, cutting, and verifying a release. Adapt to the project's actual tooling; the names below assume git, GitHub CLI, and a Node/Python toolchain.

commands.command: gh pr list --base main --state merged --search "merged:>$(git log -1 --format=%aI $(git describe --tags --abbrev=0))"
git tag -a v$VERSION -m "release v$VERSION" && git push origin v$VERSION
git revert --no-commit v$VERSION..HEAD && git commit -m "revert: roll back v$VERSION"

section.name: prompt-templates

prompt.purpose: Draft a release plan from a CHANGELOG and milestone state.

section.prompt:
You are preparing release vX.Y.Z. Inputs: - CHANGELOG.md [Unreleased] section. - Open milestone "vX.Y.Z" with N closed issues. - Current branch state and last tag.
Produce a release plan with these sections: 1. Goal (one sentence outcome). 2. Success metric (number, target, deadline). 3. Contents: features, fixes, docs, breaking changes (each links to PR/issue). 4. Dependencies and risks. 5. Rollback plan (one-command path that has been tested in staging). 6. Communication plan: release notes, email, blog, social, in-app, status page. 7. Timeline: freeze date, staging deploy, production deploy window, monitor window.
Hard constraints: - Use SemVer 2.0 numbering and explain MAJOR/MINOR/PATCH choice. - No Friday production deploys. - Mark every breaking change with a migration note. - Output Markdown, no time estimates ("after step X" instead of "in 2h").

prompt.purpose: Generate customer-facing release notes from a technical PR list.

section.prompt:
You are writing customer-facing release notes for vX.Y.Z. Input: list of merged PRs with title, body, and labels.
Output a Markdown document with sections in this order: - Highlights (1-3 bullets, plain language, value-focused). - New features (group by area; include screenshots/links if linked in PRs). - Improvements. - Bug fixes (one-line each). - Breaking changes (each: what changed, why, migration steps, deprecation timeline). - Security (CVE IDs if applicable). - Acknowledgements (external contributors).
Style: - Active voice, second person ("you can now"). - No internal jargon; expand acronyms on first use. - Link every change to its PR or issue. - Lead with user-visible value, not implementation detail.

prompt.purpose: Run the readiness gate as an automated check.

section.prompt:
You are the release readiness gate for vX.Y.Z. For each of the seven items below, return PASS or FAIL with a one-sentence justification grounded in the linked artifact. If any item is FAIL, refuse the release.
Items: 1. Code complete — all milestone PRs merged, CI green. 2. Tests passing — unit + integration + e2e green within last 24h. 3. Docs updated — README, API ref, CHANGELOG [Unreleased] -> [vX.Y.Z], migration notes. 4. Migrations tested — applied + rolled back on prod-shape DB. 5. Rollback plan — one-command path documented and dry-run-validated. 6. Support briefed — runbook delivered ≥24h before deploy. 7. Release notes — customer-facing draft reviewed by one peer.
Output: Markdown table | Item | Status | Evidence link | Notes |. Final line: READINESS=PASS or READINESS=FAIL.

section.name: changelog-template
section.p: Follow Keep a Changelog 1.1.0. Maintain CHANGELOG.md at repo root. On each release, rename `[Unreleased]` to `[vX.Y.Z] - YYYY-MM-DD` and create a fresh empty `[Unreleased]` block above it.
section.code:
# Changelog All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file. The format is based on Keep a Changelog 1.1.0 and this project adheres to SemVer 2.0.
## [Unreleased] ### Added ### Changed ### Deprecated ### Removed ### Fixed ### Security
## [1.4.0] - 2026-05-15 ### Added - Real-time collaboration on tasks (#412). ### Fixed - Fix payment retry loop on 402 (#420).
[Unreleased]: https://github.com/org/repo/compare/v1.4.0...HEAD [1.4.0]: https://github.com/org/repo/compare/v1.3.0...v1.4.0

section.name: rollback-plan-template
section.p: Every release ships with a written rollback plan. Store at `docs/releases/vX.Y.Z-rollback.md` and dry-run it on staging before approving the release.
section.code:
# Rollback Plan v1.4.0
## Trigger conditions - 5xx rate > 1% sustained 5 minutes. - p95 latency > 2x baseline sustained 10 minutes. - Critical user-reported regression (data loss, payment failure).
## Owner - Primary: @alice (on-call until +24h post-deploy). - Backup: @bob.
## Procedure (target time-to-restore: < 10 minutes) 1. Announce in #incidents: "rolling back v1.4.0". 2. Run: `kubectl rollout undo deployment/api --to-revision=$PREV`. 3. Verify smoke: `curl -fsS https://example.com/healthz`. 4. Confirm error rate < 0.1% for 5 minutes. 5. Run: `git revert --no-commit v1.4.0..HEAD && git commit -m "revert: v1.4.0"`. 6. Update status page: incident resolved. 7. Post-mortem within 48h.
## Data migration reversal - v1.4.0 migration `0042_add_team_table` is forward-only. - Data written by new code is compatible with v1.3.x readers (validated in staging). - If reversal is required, run `python manage.py migrate app 0041` then redeploy v1.3.x.

antipattern.reason: Weekend on-call has reduced coverage and slower mean-time-to-restore. A Monday-Wednesday production window keeps the full team available for the critical 24-hour monitoring period.
Every release must have a one-command rollback path that has been dry-run on staging. Without it, incident response time multiplies and pressure leads to poor decisions.

antipattern.description: Last-minute feature additions after the freeze.
antipattern.reason: Late additions skip the readiness checklist and break the test baseline. Enforce a 24-48 hour code freeze before every release; later changes go to the next release.

antipattern.description: Skipping communication because the release is "just a patch".
antipattern.reason: Users and support staff notice changes regardless of size. Silent releases generate confused tickets. Always publish at least a CHANGELOG entry and an in-app changelog link.
Staging catches integration issues that unit tests miss. Mandatory for any release touching data migrations, third-party integrations, or infrastructure.
Error rate, latency, and conversion metrics can degrade subtly after a release. Watch for at least 24 hours before declaring success and updating the status page.

antipattern.description: Monster releases bundling months of features.
antipattern.reason: Large releases have higher risk, harder rollbacks, and diluted communication impact. Smaller, more frequent releases reduce blast radius and keep users engaged with steady visible progress.
A premature announcement followed by a delay or rollback erodes trust faster than any feature can rebuild it. Verify smoke + 30 minutes of clean metrics before publishing the announcement.

example.title: v2.4.0 — team collaboration release
example.note: Goal: enable team collaboration. Metric: 20% of active users use a team feature within 7 days of release.
Contents: real-time collaboration, comments on tasks, 15 fixes. SemVer: MINOR (additive features only).
Timeline: Mon freeze + regression run, Wed staging deploy + final QA, Thu 14:00 UTC production deploy, Thu 15:00 UTC announcement (after 30-min metric watch).
Rollback: `kubectl rollout undo deployment/api`, owner on-call until Fri 14:00 UTC.

example.title: v1.2.3 — payment hotfix
example.note: Goal: fix critical payment processing bug. Metric: zero payment failures with the new code path for 24h.
Contents: payment fix, 2 minor UI bugs. SemVer: PATCH.
Timeline: same-day staging smoke at 11:00, deploy at 13:00, monitor until 14:00, changelog + email to affected customers only.
Rollback: revert PR #218 + redeploy previous tag; tested on staging at 10:30.

example.title: Friday 17:00 deploy with no rollback plan
example.note: v3.0.0 (MAJOR) shipped 17:00 Friday with breaking auth change.
Bug discovered 18:30; on-call solo engineer; rollback plan undocumented.
Restore-time 4h; 23% of weekend logins failed; trust hit visible in churn for two weeks.
Lessons: enforce no-Friday rule, mandate documented + dry-run-tested rollback for every MAJOR.

verify.step: Run: `git tag -l "v$VERSION"` returns the tag and `git push --tags` succeeded.
Run: `gh release view v$VERSION --json isDraft,publishedAt` shows `isDraft=false` and a non-null timestamp.
Confirm CHANGELOG has `[vX.Y.Z] - YYYY-MM-DD` block populated and `[Unreleased]` reset to empty headers.
Confirm error rate (5xx) for the new version is < 0.1% sustained over 30 minutes after deploy.
Confirm p95 latency is within 1.2x of the 7-day baseline 30 minutes after deploy.
Confirm release notes are published, customer email sent, in-app banner live.
Schedule and complete the 30-minute retrospective within 72 hours; capture three actions in the next-release backlog.

Verify DORA metrics for this release: deploy frequency unchanged or up, lead-time-for-change recorded, change-failure-rate marked false (or true with linked incident), MTTR recorded if any incident occurred.

list.item: Goal sentence, success metric, and SemVer-correct version are written before any deploy command runs.
All seven readiness checks pass; readiness gate prompt returns READINESS=PASS.
Rollback plan is written, owned, and dry-run-validated on staging.
Each identified audience received its asset on or before deploy.
24-hour monitoring window completed; error rate and latency within thresholds.
Retrospective held; learnings captured in roadmap or backlog.

template.purpose: Full release plan: metadata, goal, contents, dependencies, risks, rollback plan, communication plan, timeline.
Release notes template: highlights, new features, improvements, bug fixes, breaking changes, security.
